cards.front: What is a perpendicular bisector of a segment?
cards.back: A line, ray, or segment that intersects a segment at its midpoint and forms a 90° angle.

cards.front: What does the Perpendicular Bisector Theorem state?
cards.back: If a point lies on the perpendicular bisector of a segment, it is equidistant from the segment's endpoints.

cards.front: What is the converse of the Perpendicular Bisector Theorem?
cards.back: If a point is equidistant from the endpoints of a segment, it lies on the perpendicular bisector of that segment.

cards.front: What is an angle bisector?
cards.back: A ray that divides an angle into two congruent (equal-measure) angles.

cards.front: What does the Angle Bisector Theorem state?
cards.back: If a point lies on the bisector of an angle, it is equidistant from the two sides of the angle.

cards.front: What is a median of a triangle?
cards.back: A segment drawn from a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side.

cards.front: What is an altitude of a triangle?
cards.back: A perpendicular segment from a vertex to the line containing the opposite side (may lie inside, on, or outside the triangle).

cards.front: What is a midsegment of a triangle?
cards.back: A segment connecting the midpoints of two sides of a triangle.

cards.front: What does the Triangle Midsegment Theorem state?
cards.back: A midsegment is parallel to the third side and exactly half its length.

cards.front: What is a point of concurrency?
cards.back: The single point where three or more lines, segments, or rays intersect.

cards.front: What is the circumcenter of a triangle?
cards.back: The point of concurrency of the three perpendicular bisectors. It is the center of the circumscribed circle (circumcircle).

cards.front: Where is the circumcenter located in acute, right, and obtuse triangles?
cards.back: Acute: inside the triangle. Right: at the midpoint of the hypotenuse. Obtuse: outside the triangle.

cards.front: What is the incenter of a triangle?
cards.back: The point of concurrency of the three angle bisectors. It is the center of the inscribed circle (incircle).

cards.front: Why is the incenter always inside the triangle?
cards.back: Because angle bisectors always lie within the interior of a triangle, regardless of its type.

cards.front: What is the centroid of a triangle?
cards.back: The point of concurrency of the three medians. It is the triangle's center of gravity or balance point.

cards.front: What does the Centroid Theorem state about median lengths?
cards.back: The centroid divides each median into two segments with a 2:1 ratio. The longer segment (from vertex to centroid) is 2/3 of the total median length.

cards.front: What is the orthocenter of a triangle?
cards.back: The point of concurrency of the three altitudes (or lines containing the altitudes).

cards.front: Where is the orthocenter located in acute, right, and obtuse triangles?
cards.back: Acute: inside. Right: at the vertex of the right angle. Obtuse: outside.

cards.front: What is the difference between a median and an altitude?
cards.back: A median connects a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. An altitude connects a vertex perpendicularly to the opposite side. They only coincide in isosceles/equilateral triangles from the vertex angle.

cards.front: What is the difference between a perpendicular bisector and an angle bisector?
cards.back: A perpendicular bisector cuts a side at 90° through its midpoint. An angle bisector cuts an interior angle into two equal parts. They are unrelated unless the triangle is equilateral.

cards.front: How do you find the coordinates of a triangle's centroid?
cards.back: Average the coordinates of the three vertices: ((x₁+x₂+x₃)/3, (y₁+y₂+y₃)/3).

cards.front: What property makes the incenter useful for finding the radius of an inscribed circle?
cards.back: It is equidistant from all three sides of the triangle. That distance is the radius of the incircle.

cards.front: What property makes the circumcenter useful for finding the radius of a circumscribed circle?
cards.back: It is equidistant from all three vertices of the triangle. That distance is the radius of the circumcircle.

cards.front: What is the Euler Line?
cards.back: A straight line that passes through the orthocenter, centroid, and circumcenter in any non-equilateral triangle.

cards.front: When do all four points of concurrency coincide at a single point?
cards.back: Only in an equilateral triangle, where all centers (incenter, circumcenter, centroid, orthocenter) are the same point.

cards.front: How many midsegments can a triangle have?
cards.back: Exactly three, each connecting a different pair of midpoints.

cards.front: How does the perimeter of the midsegment triangle compare to the original triangle?
cards.back: It is exactly half the perimeter of the original triangle.

cards.front: How do altitudes behave in an obtuse triangle?
cards.back: Two altitudes must be extended outside the triangle to intersect the lines containing the opposite sides.

cards.front: What geometric construction creates a perpendicular bisector?
cards.back: Draw congruent arcs from both endpoints of a segment using a compass, then connect the two arc intersection points.

cards.front: Why are special segments important in real-world engineering and design?
cards.back: They help locate structural centers of balance (centroid), optimal support points (orthocenter), and evenly distribute forces or materials across triangular frameworks.
